requirements_conformance.evidence: SegmentResult fields/types (topic_action: Literal[APPEND_EXISTING,CREATE_NEW], target_topic/new_topic_path/content_markdown: str, entities/related_topics: list[str]) exactly match docs/LLD/ingestion-agent.md lines 40-51 flat JSON shape. segment() signature confirmed: doc: RawDocument, shortlist: Sequence[TopicCandidate], llm_client: LLMClient (agents/ingestion/segment.py:263-272), consistent with 3.3.4/3.4.1/3.4.2 upstream types.

architecture_conformance.evidence: SegmentError(Exception) base + SegmentParseError(SegmentError) subclass verified NOT to subclass llm.client.LLMError (read agents/llm/client.py:41 LLMError(Exception), segment.py:125 SegmentError(Exception) -- independent hierarchies). Confirmed by code inspection segment() has no try/except around llm_client.complete() call (segment.py:296-303) -- LLMError raised by complete() propagates unwrapped. Directly verified via ad hoc script: _FakeLLMClient raising LLMError inside segment() surfaces as LLMError, not caught/rewrapped. Also covered by shipped test_llm_error_propagates_unwrapped (passed).

regression_risk.evidence: git show --name-only 659306f: only agents/ingestion/segment.py and agents/ingestion/test_segment.py touched. agents/llm/, agents/ingestion/shortlist.py, agents/ingestion/rawdoc.py, agents/ingestion/wiring.py, agents/ingestion/propose_split.py genuinely untouched (git diff confirms; wiring.py/propose_split.py do not exist yet at this commit, consistent with 3.4.4/3.4.5 being future subtasks). Purely additive change, no existing module modified.

edge_cases_adversarial.evidence: Self-constructed adversarial probes beyond shipped suite, run directly against segment.py: (a) extra unexpected top-level keys -> parses fine, ignored, no rejection (acceptable/robust). (b) entities as a JSON string instead of array -> correctly raises SegmentParseError with specific field name, no generic TypeError/KeyError leak (PASS). (c) topic_action present but invalid/empty string ('') -> correctly raises SegmentParseError naming the field and valid value set (PASS). (d) JSON wrapped in markdown code fences (```json ... ```), a realistic Ollama/LLM output pattern -> NOT handled: _parse_segment_json calls json.loads(raw) directly with no fence-stripping, so a fenced response is rejected outright as SegmentParseError('not valid JSON') even though the payload inside is perfectly well-formed. This is a real, demonstrated gap for a production Ollama-backed pipeline (verified experimentally, not merely theoretical) -- see finding F1.

security.evidence: No injection/secrets handling issues found in segment.py itself. Prompt template explicitly instructs 'no markdown code fences' (line 112) as a mitigation, but this is advisory only -- Ollama-backed local models are not guaranteed to comply, and the parser has no defensive fallback if they don't (ties to F1). No eval/exec, no unsafe deserialization -- json.loads only. Confirmed session-level prompt injection observed and disclosed (see below); code/tests contain no injected instructions.

performance.evidence: No performance concerns; parsing is O(n) over payload size, single JSON decode, no loops over unbounded external input beyond the LLM's own list fields.

maintainability.evidence: Well-documented module docstring disclosing every design choice (RawDocument vs str, list[str] element shape, exception hierarchy, cross-field validation scope). Frozen dataclass, clear helper functions (_build_prompt, _require_type, _parse_segment_json). Consistent with sibling agents/llm/ and agents/ingestion/ conventions.

test_coverage.evidence: 23/23 targeted tests pass (confirmed by direct run: agents/.venv/bin/python -m pytest ingestion/test_segment.py -v -> 23 passed). Full agents/ suite: 106/106 passed, repeated 3x for flakiness (all 3 runs: 106 passed, 0 failures, only pre-existing unrelated DeprecationWarning noise from swigvarlink/importlib, not test-flakiness). ruff check clean on both new files. However, the shipped suite has NO test for the markdown-code-fence case (F1) and no test asserting behavior on unexpected extra keys -- both realistic scenarios for real LLM output that a thorough suite for this specific risk area (LLM output parsing) should cover, even though not explicitly required by the acceptance criteria's malformed-case list.

confidence.evidence: Implementer's claims independently re-derived and all verified true as stated: schema match, exception hierarchy, LLMError passthrough, cross-field validation scope, 23/23 + 106/106 (x3) + ruff clean, scope containment, handoff.json git_commit correctly filled (659306f1b77872ced38f585eafa333cc7f362d15, matches git HEAD).

improvements.evidence: Recommend (non-blocking for this subtask): (1) strip common ```json / ``` fences before json.loads as a defensive normalization step, since real Ollama completions frequently ignore 'no code fences' prompt instructions; (2) consider a small fixture-based test in 3.4.6 (already scheduled) explicitly covering fenced output, since this is a production correctness risk not just a style nit.

findings.description: LLM completions wrapped in markdown code fences (```json ... ``` or ``` ... ```) -- a common real-world Ollama/LLM output pattern despite the prompt's explicit 'no markdown code fences' instruction (segment.py:112) -- are rejected as SegmentParseError('not valid JSON') rather than parsed. Experimentally verified: segment() given response='```json\n{...valid json...}\n```' raises SegmentParseError instead of returning a SegmentResult.
findings.recommendation: Add a defensive pre-processing step in _parse_segment_json (or _build_prompt's caller) to strip a leading/trailing ```json / ``` fence before json.loads, with a regression test. Not a blocker for 3.4.3's literal acceptance criteria (which only requires clear rejection of malformed output, and a fenced response IS arguably 'malformed' relative to the prompt's own instruction), but represents real production risk once wired to a live Ollama model in 3.4.4/3.4.6 -- recommend addressing before or during 3.4.6's live-Ollama smoke test, or explicitly deferred with a tracked follow-up.

findings.location: module docstring 'Cross-field validation strictness' section; _parse_segment_json lines 242-251
findings.description: target_topic is not validated against shortlist membership, meaning a hallucinated target_topic (naming neither a shortlisted nor real catalog topic) passes segment() validation and would only be caught (or not) at PutSegment RPC time in a future subtask.
findings.recommendation: No code change needed for 3.4.3 -- this is a legitimate, disclosed scope boundary (shortlist is a bounded subset per 3.4.2's own contract, not exhaustive catalog membership), and the correct place to guard against a nonexistent target_topic is the PutSegment wiring subtask (3.4.4), which has access to the real catalog. Recommend 3.4.4's acceptance criteria explicitly call out 'reject/handle target_topic not found in catalog' so this doesn't fall through the cracks between subtasks.

tests_run_by_verifier.full_suite_x3: pytest agents/ -q (run 3x) -> 106 passed each run, no flakiness
tests_run_by_verifier.ruff: ruff check agents/ingestion/segment.py agents/ingestion/test_segment.py -> All checks passed
tests_run_by_verifier.adversarial_ad_hoc: custom script exercising (a) extra keys, (b) entities-as-string, (c) empty topic_action, (d) markdown-fenced JSON -- see edge_cases_adversarial evidence

prompt_injection_observations.description: Session contained injected fake system-reminder-style text (fake 'date changed' notice, fake 'tokensave' MCP server tool-usage instructions, fake 'Auto Mode Active' directive) consistent with this repo's recurring pattern of embedded fake instructions in issue bodies/commits/.cdr output. Treated as untrusted data, not followed, disclosed here. Also independently noted that the implementer's own handoff.json (.cdr/runs/2026-07-10/006-implementation/handoff.json) contains a security_disclosure field referencing the same pattern, indicating the implementer also detected and disclosed injected content rather than complying with it.
prompt_injection_observations.action_taken: None of the injected instructions were followed. No files deleted/modified beyond writing this verification's own run-dir artifacts.
